cue: How do you say hardly anyone showed up?

es.meaning: To be hardly anyone; a very thin crowd.
es.example: Fuimos al estreno y éramos cuatro gatos.
es.exampleTranslation: We went to the opening and there was hardly a soul.
es.regionNote: Also 'había cuatro gatos'. Reviewer to confirm MX currency.

it.meaning: There to be hardly anyone; a very thin crowd.
it.example: Siamo andati alla prima ed eravamo quattro gatti.
it.exampleTranslation: We went to the opening and there was hardly a soul.

bridge.note: The freakiest twin in the bank: same animal, same number, same shrug. Both languages independently settled on FOUR CATS as the unit of a disappointing turnout — not three, not a handful, and not dogs. When two languages agree this precisely the interesting question flips: not what diverges, but whether one borrowed it from the other, or whether four cats is simply what an empty room looks like around the Mediterranean.

interference.es_to_it: This one transfers whole — but mind the frame: Italian builds it with esserci (eravamo quattro gatti), not with avere.
interference.it_to_es: Equally safe coming back. Spanish uses ser or haber: éramos cuatro gatos, había cuatro gatos.

review.notes: ES side discovered in a Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; every gloss and example here authored fresh). IT counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Exact-twin entry whose payload is the coincidence itself — a good early-week freebie.
